title: Ray Data 2.56: Improving Reliability for AI Data Pipelines | Anyscale
gist: Ray Data 2.56 targets the two dominant reliability killers in large-scale AI batch-inference and training-ingest pipelines - OOM crashes and object spilling - with memory-aware scheduling and smarter prefetching, delivering benchmark gains like 300+ OOMs to zero, 2.4x faster runtime, and 25% higher training throughput.

insights.claim: Out-of-memory failures and unnecessary object spilling were the two most-reported reliability challenges for Ray Data users, signaling that memory management, not raw compute, is the binding constraint in large-scale AI data pipelines.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: AI-infra investment thesis: reliability/memory tooling is where enterprise pipeline value is being won, not just throughput.
insights.quote: we focused on two of the biggest reliability challenges users reported: out-of-memory (OOM) failures and unnecessary object spilling
insights.locator: Ray Data is one of the most common

insights.claim: Ray Data 2.56 makes Ray itself, rather than the underlying operating system, handle OOM process termination so it can kill idle workers instead of critical driver processes, preventing node deaths and cascading pipeline crashes.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: A moat detail: OS-level OOM killers destroy jobs; owning the termination policy is a differentiator hard for naive frameworks to match.
insights.quote: Ray also now handles OOM process termination itself rather than leaving it to the underlying operating system.
insights.locator: We’ve also improved Ray’s process management

insights.claim: An audio transcription benchmark on 8 g6.xlarge AWS instances saw OOMs fall from 300+ to 0 and end-to-end runtime drop from 1055s to 447s moving from Ray Data 2.55 to 2.56, roughly a 2.4x speedup.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Concrete cost/latency proof point vendors can cite when comparing distributed-inference stacks.
insights.quote: Going from 2.55 to 2.56, OOMs dropped from 300+ to 0, and end-to-end runtime fell from 1055s to 447s.
insights.locator: To measure the impact of the changes

insights.claim: Ray Data now auto-tunes batch sizes when passed batch_size="auto", profiling row sizes to keep inputs under a 16MB safe threshold, removing manual tuning as a source of OOMs.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Lower operational skill barrier widens the addressable user base for the framework.
insights.quote: the system profiles row sizes and automatically chooses a batch size such that the size of the inputs is within a safe threshold (in this case, under 16mb)
insights.locator: We also introduced a batch size tuning feature

insights.claim: Ray Data 2.56 defaults map tasks to 4GB of logical memory per CPU and reserves fixed memory overhead for system processes like the raylet, treating memory oversubscription as the root cause of OOM crashes.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Shows the fix philosophy: conservative memory reservation over aggressive packing, trading density for reliability.
insights.quote: logical memory configured to 4GB per CPU

insights.claim: Ray Data consolidated its internal block formats to use PyArrow as the only format, dropping Pandas DataFrames because Pandas could not accurately account for in-memory size of generic Python objects, causing backpressure to misfire and trigger spilling.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Signals PyArrow's dominance as the columnar substrate for AI data infra; Pandas being deprecated internally is directional.
insights.quote: we’ve consolidated block formats and now use PyArrow as the only block format, which has a much more accurate in-memory size estimation.

insights.claim: In an internal object store memory backpressure stress test, spilling was eliminated entirely in 2.56 (down from 70GiB in 2.55) and peak object store memory was reduced by 41%.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Eliminating disk spilling removes out-of-disk crash risk and cuts the storage footprint of large training pipelines.
insights.quote: spilling in 2.56 was eliminated (from 70GiB in 2.55), and peak object store memory reduced by 41%

insights.claim: Ray Data was over-prefetching batches by multiple times the configured prefetch_batches per worker with no throughput benefit, and this untracked pinned data was a hidden cause of object-store underestimation and spilling.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Illustrates that naive prefetch heuristics silently inflate memory; watch whether competing dataloaders share this flaw.
insights.quote: Ray Data prefetched multiple times the number of configured prefetch_batches per worker despite not having any improvement to throughput

themes.theme: GPU stream contention and training throughput

insights.claim: Prefetching data from CPU to GPU was contending with the default CUDA stream, queuing compute kernels and lowering training throughput, so reducing unnecessary prefetch batches yielded a 25% training-step throughput increase in a dataloading-bottlenecked benchmark.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: CUDA stream contention is a subtle GPU-utilization tax; dataloader design directly moves expensive GPU throughput.
insights.quote: we actually saw a 25% training step throughput increase due to reducing CUDA stream contention from prefetching fewer unnecessary batches to GPU

insights.claim: Ray Data addressed scheduling-loop latency degradation at high worker counts, cutting P90 scheduling-loop latency by up to 6x at 2000+ workers.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Scaling to 2000+ workers signals the customer profile Anyscale targets - very large training clusters.
insights.quote: scheduling loop P90 latencies drop by up to 6x at large scale (2000+ workers)

insights.claim: Ray Data 2.56 adds multiple-dataset support with subcluster label scheduling, enabling running validation alongside training, dataset multi-tenancy, and keeping preprocessing workers off training GPU nodes.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Isolating CPU preprocessing from GPU nodes protects the most expensive resource; a selling point for cost-conscious ML teams.
insights.quote: keeping training-dataset preprocessing workers off your training GPU nodes

insights.claim: Local shuffle buffer improvements reduced memory usage by up to 2.5x and delivered up to 3x higher throughput on a variety of training-shuffle workloads.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Shuffle is a chronic memory/throughput bottleneck in training ingest; multiplicative gains here compound cluster efficiency.
insights.quote: reduce memory usage by up to 2.5x and 3x higher throughput on a variety of workloads

insights.claim: Ray Data 2.56 only mitigated prefetch accounting; a full root-cause fix, plus mid-epoch resumption, Datasource V2 with better schema inference, and fault-tolerant shuffles, are slated for 2.57.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Roadmap shows fault tolerance and checkpoint recovery are the next competitive battleground for training-ingest infra.
insights.quote: Mid-epoch resumption for faster checkpoint-based recovery for training workloads

insights.claim: Anyscale positions Ray Data as one of the most common solutions for large-scale batch inference and training ingest, framing the product around production-AI workloads across multi-cloud.
insights.speaker: Balaji Veeramani
insights.so_what: Market-positioning claim to gauge Anyscale's competitive standing in the distributed AI data-processing category.
insights.quote: Ray Data is one of the most common solutions for large-scale batch inference and training ingest pipelines.
insights.locator: Ray Data is one of the most common
